reconstructed_sketch: From chapter context alone (2.20-2.25 imperishable, 2.27-2.30 closing), one could predict that 2.26 would be a transitional verse continuing the imperishable theme. One would NOT predict the specific concessive-conditional structure ('even if you reject... still'), the materialist premise being granted, or the engineering analog grounded in the secular argument from the engineer's own commit history. The verse contributes specifically the concessive-shape argument, which is doctrinally distinct from the assertive shape of 2.20-2.25.
verse_specific_signal: The conditional concession — 'even on the materialist reading where code is only code, this Kafka consumer was always going to become this Kafka consumer's replacement' — anchors specifically on atha ca... tathāpi rather than on the imperishable-doctrine claim of the surrounding block. This is the argumentative structure no other verse in the chapter contributes.

overlap_with_other_verses.explanation: Both verses share the deprecation-management tag, but 2.26 contributes the concessive structure and 2.27 contributes the structural-inevitability argument; they are paired, not redundant.

verdict_reason: The verse-specific signal — that grief fails even if the imperishable doctrine is rejected — is the precise argument 2.26 makes that the rest of the block does not. The engineering layer anchors on this concessive shape (the 'even on the secular premise' clause) rather than re-stating the imperishable thesis.
